services.title: Design and development that sells.
services.lede: Design, development, and visual content — all in one seamless process.
services.01.title: Brand identity & graphic design
services.01.text: Logo, color palette, and a complete visual system. A brand that's instantly recognizable and feels cohesive across every touchpoint.
services.02.title: Web design & development
services.02.text: Fast, custom-built websites — designed and coded by the same person. No templates, no handoffs. PageSpeed 97 on real client projects.
services.03.title: Content & social media design
services.03.text: Visual assets for social that stop the scroll and build brand equity — crafted with intention, not assembled from a template.
why.title:
The difference isn't price.
It's speed.
why.lede: An agency puts three people between your idea and the result: the salesperson, the project manager, and the person who actually does the work. Every layer adds time and dilutes what you asked for. With me, there are no layers — you talk directly to the person designing and building your project.
why.them.01: You talk to a salesperson; the work is done by someone you never meet
why.them.02: Your brief passes through 3 people before it becomes actual work
why.them.03: Revisions and changes take days to coordinate
why.them.04: Design and code are handled by different teams — things get lost in handoff
why.them.05: You're also paying for the overhead: office, managers, commissions
why.me.01: You talk directly to the person designing and building your project
why.me.02: What you ask for arrives without anyone diluting it in the middle
why.me.03: A change gets resolved the same day, not next week
why.me.04: Design and code are done by the same person — nothing gets lost
why.me.05: You pay for the work, not the structure around it
why.close: It's not that agencies are bad. It's that for most projects, that structure is overhead you pay for and time you wait through — without getting a better result.
focus.title:
Clear thinking.
Real results.
focus.lede:
Design. Development. Content.
All in one person.
process.title: From brief to delivery.
process.01.title: We talk
process.01.text: A 20-minute call or a short form. You tell me what you need, who your customer is, and what you want to achieve. No decks, no endless meetings — that's enough to know if we're a good fit.
process.02.title: Proposal in 48 h
process.02.text: Within two days you receive a document with the exact scope: what's included, what isn't, delivery timeline, and price. We review it together and adjust anything before signing.
process.03.title: Production with visibility
process.03.text: I don't disappear for two weeks and show up with something finished. I work in partial deliveries so you can see real progress, give feedback in time, and the final result is never a surprise.
process.04.title: Ready to use
process.04.text: Organized files, clean code, and everything you need to take the project from there. No dependency on me. And if you need a tweak in the first 7 days, we handle it at no extra cost.

about.bio.02: For 2+ years I've managed everything digital at Orbidental: e-commerce, Meta and Google Ads, product photography, and design. Not theory — real environments, real metrics.

testimonials.title:
What they say
about my work.
testimonials.01.text: Alexander gave us a visual identity far more powerful than we expected. He captured exactly the essence of fire and set us completely apart from other food brands. People recognize us instantly now — the design is unique and speaks for itself.
testimonials.01.name: Felipe Romero
testimonials.02.text: The result exceeded our expectations. Alexander understood exactly what we wanted: the feel of a jungle, but unmistakably a nightclub. Every piece reflects the name and energy of the brand.
testimonials.02.name: Mayra Castaño
testimonials.03.text: We couldn't be happier with the result. We wanted something urban, with that graffiti edge but applied to streetwear — and Alexander got it from the very first conversation. The identity came out exactly as we imagined it.
testimonials.03.name: Steven Mesa
trusted.label:
Brands that
have trusted me
faq.title:
Things people
always ask.
faq.lede: Clear answers so you can make confident decisions and we can work with full transparency.
faq.01.q: Do you work with clients outside Colombia?
faq.01.a: Yes — I work 100% remotely and have clients in multiple countries. We coordinate via WhatsApp, Zoom, or whatever channel you prefer. Time zones haven't been an issue. I'm in Bogotá (UTC-5) and I adapt to my clients' schedules.
faq.02.a: I work with a 50% deposit to kick things off and the remaining 50% on delivery. For larger projects we split it into milestones. For international clients I accept PayPal or Wise.
faq.03.a: It depends on the service, but generally I include two rounds of revisions within the agreed scope. If you need more after that, we price it separately — no surprises. What matters is that the final result is right.
faq.04.q: What time zone do you work in?
faq.04.a: I'm in Bogotá, Colombia — UTC-5, no daylight saving. I'm usually available between 8am and 8pm Colombian time, but I'm flexible when we need to schedule a call across time zones.
faq.05.a: Always. Before starting any project we sign an agreement that defines the scope, timeline, price, and delivery terms. It protects both of us and prevents misunderstandings down the road.
faq.06.q: What if I need changes after delivery?
faq.06.a: Minor changes within the original scope are covered at no extra cost if you request them within the first 7 days. After that, or for significant direction changes, we price them separately — always with a clear quote before touching anything.

tw.hero.sub: Every brand on this page was losing something — sales, recognition, trust — before I came in. Below, what happened after.
tw.stat1.label: PageSpeed, Orbidental
tw.stat2.label: products in a B2B catalog
tw.stat3.label: middlemen between you and the code
tw.orbidental.lead: A dental supply distributor sizes up their vendor in seconds. If the site loads slow and breaks on mobile, they assume the whole operation is like that — and they leave for a competitor.
tw.orbidental.metric2: SEO or content lost in migration
tw.orbidental.detail: I migrated a heavy Divi site to clean code, preserving all existing rankings. Now I'm connecting the catalog to their inventory system via API — code-level integration, not a plugin.
tw.pulse.problem: The challenge: prove I don't repeat a single style. Fictional brand, 100% real code and deployment.
tw.pulse.solution: The call: different language, different palette, different typeface — no frameworks, no dependencies. Vanilla, live.
tw.acelerarte.problem: The challenge: motorcycle racing is intense to live, but its content almost always looks generic.
tw.acelerarte.solution: The call: my own motorsport flyer brand — competition-grade aesthetics that recognized riders actually repost.
tw.fueggo.problem: The challenge: the brand looked different at the restaurant, on packaging, and on uniforms — nothing tied together.
tw.fueggo.solution: The call: a complete system — logo, palette, typography — applied to real, print-ready pieces.
tw.jungla.problem: The challenge: an intense nightlife concept — masked DJs, jungle aesthetic — with no identity to match.
tw.jungla.solution: The call: a system that hits just as hard on a flyer, a facade, and a uniform.
tw.rebel.problem: The challenge: in a saturated streetwear market, you're either instantly recognizable or invisible.
tw.rebel.solution: The call: a complete system, from logo to packaging, built so every piece is unmistakable.
tw.cap.title: Every project goes through one person

orb.hero.sub: A catalog of 3,000+ dental products that took 14 seconds to load and broke on mobile. Customers leaving before seeing a single price. I rebuilt it from scratch — without losing a single product or a single Google ranking — and took PageSpeed from 40 to 97.
orb.metric1: PageSpeed Score — Core Web Vitals passed in one month
orb.metric2: How long it took to load, before
orb.metric4: Responsive — before, it didn't work on mobile
orb.problem.title: A slow website isn't a technical problem. It's a sales problem.
orb.problem.p1: The previous site was built on Divi: it dragged over a megabyte of unused code on every visit, wasn't designed for mobile, and — honestly — looked bad. Flat background, scattered photos, no visual hierarchy. It took 14 seconds to open.
orb.problem.pull: Every second of waiting and every broken screen on a phone was a customer closing the tab.
orb.problem.p2: This was happening on a 3,000+ product catalog where people arrive looking for something specific and in a hurry. For a business selling online, that doesn't show up in the design — it shows up in the sales that never come in.
orb.problem.p3: Divi couldn't be "optimized" — the weight was in the system itself. It had to go. I rebuilt everything in clean code on WordPress, migrated all 3,000+ products without losing existing SEO rankings — one mistake there and months of positioning collapse — and redesigned the visual experience so the site finally conveyed the seriousness of the company.
orb.info.company.label: Company
orb.info.role.label: My role
orb.info.role.value: In charge of all digital · 2+ years
orb.info.category.label: Category
orb.info.stack.label: Stack
orb.info.deliverables.label: Deliverables
orb.info.deliverables.value: Full redesign, performance, technical SEO, responsive
orb.proof.title: From "Failed" to "Passed" in one month.
orb.proof.text: This isn't my opinion — it's Google's own Core Web Vitals report. In one month the site went from "Failed" to "Passed", and PageSpeed climbed from 40 to 97. In practice: 14 seconds of load time turned into a near-instant open.
orb.proof.before.label: Before
orb.proof.after.label: After
orb.showcase.title: Homepage
orb.showcase.text: Before: flat background, no hierarchy, slow. Now: a coherent, organized homepage that looks professional on any screen and loads instantly. A company's credibility is designed too.
orb.gallery1.title: Brand page
orb.gallery1.text: Eighteeth is one of the brands Orbidental distributes. Instead of another generic page, I built a dedicated experience inside the site — proof that this doesn't come from a template, but is designed brand by brand.
orb.gallery2.title: The prototype for the future store
orb.gallery2.text: The current product pages are basic — white background, one photo, unstructured text. The real e-commerce is now in development. To define where it's headed and align the team, I designed this prototype of how the product page will look and work: clear, coherent, and built so customers understand what they're buying and feel confident deciding. This is Orbidental's real next step — and I'm the one building it.
orb.cta.title: Think your site is fast enough
orb.cta.sub: Open it on a phone with mobile data, away from your home network. That's where Orbidental was losing customers without knowing it — and where most stores that "look fine" lose theirs too. If you're not sure, you're probably already losing them. Tell me what you sell and within 24 hours I'll tell you exactly what your current site is costing you.
pd.hero.sub: A premium agency identity built from scratch — in English, for international clients, without a single element borrowed from my own brand.
pd.challenge.title: An international client doesn't ask if I can design — they ask if I can step outside my style.
pd.challenge.p1: My entire portfolio lives in the same visual universe: dark, gold, Fraunces, one voice. That's brand consistency — but for a client looking for a premium English-language marketing agency, that universe doesn't work as a reference. They need to see that I can build a completely different identity with the same level of craft.
pd.challenge.pull: Pulse Digital is that proof: a fictional agency brand built to demonstrate real versatility — not just "I can change colors" but I can switch visual languages entirely and have it feel intentional, not generic.
pd.info.client.label: Client
pd.info.lang.label: Language
pd.result.title: An agency that feels like a consultancy, not another SaaS.
pd.result.text: The final direction was a modern growth/SaaS identity: Poppins typography, violet and lime green as accents on white, and real team photography instead of just text in columns. This is a preview of the site — click to open it in full.
pd.mock.caption: Preview — click anywhere to open the full site in a new tab.
pd.hard.title: Leaving my palette isn't just changing the hex.
pd.hard.text: Anyone can invert colors and call it "another identity". The real challenge was building a system with the same internal logic any real brand has: a typeface with its own personality, a palette where the accent appears with intention, and photography that adds real visual weight — not a generic template with a color swap.
pd.tokens.type.desc: Geometric, rounded — distinct from the serif and Space Grotesk of my own portfolio
pd.tokens.concept.text: Modern growth agency — real photography, bento grid, integrated metrics
pd.solved.label: What I solved
pd.solved.01.title: Composition with real visual weight, not just text in columns
pd.solved.01.desc: Early directions were solid typographically but felt flat: everything stacked in even columns. I researched real references (Awwwards-winning agencies) and the difference was in large-scale photography and asymmetric composition — I applied that to the hero, services, and results bento.
pd.solved.02.title: Photography curation with verified licensing
pd.solved.02.desc: Every image was individually selected and verified for licensing and composition before use — no generic stock banks left unchecked. The result: real team, real dashboard, an active brand feel rather than random stock photos.
pd.solved.03.title: English copy with genuine authority
pd.solved.03.desc: No generic "we help brands grow". The copy was written with a serious agency voice: specific, direct, with honest admissions of limits ("we'll tell you honestly whether we're the right fit") — that builds more trust than any results claim.
pd.cta.title: Does your brand need an identity that doesn't feel generic
pd.cta.sub: Tell me about your project. I reply within 24 hours.
br.gallery.title: The system, piece by piece
br.info.tools.label: Tools
fg.diag.label: Before opening / ready to open
fg.hero.sub: Fueggo was about to open with no identity at all — no logo, no palette, no system. A grilled food brand on the verge of competing against dozens of nearly identical spots, with nothing to make it recognizable. I built the complete brand system so it stands out before anyone reads the name.
fg.problem.title: Without an identity, a new restaurant doesn't compete. It disappears.
fg.problem.p1: Fueggo didn't have a location open yet when they came to me. They had a clear product — burgers cooked over live charcoal, with that flavor only direct fire gives — but no visual face. No logo, no colors, not a single piece that said "this is Fueggo" before someone read the name.
fg.problem.pull: In fast grilled food, customers decide in seconds between nearly identical options. Without a strong identity, Fueggo was going to open competing blind.
fg.problem.p2: The brief wasn't "make a nice logo". It was translating the product concept — fire, charcoal, intensity, speed — into a visual system that worked equally well on a uniform, a packaging box, and a storefront, and that any print vendor could apply without losing consistency.
fg.info.role: Brand identity from zero to complete system
fg.info.deliverables: Logo, brand guidelines, storefront, packaging, uniforms, stationery, stickers
fg.decision.title: Every line had to smell like charcoal
fg.decision.p1: I didn't design a logo and then "apply it" to everything else. I started backwards: what does someone need to feel when they see a Fueggo box in hand, or the uniform of the person delivering their order? The answer — fire, character, speed — drove every decision: the typeface had to look sharp and fast, the palette had to evoke grill and charcoal without falling into the generic red-yellow of "fast food", and the symbol had to work as small as a sticker and as large as a storefront.
fg.decision.p2: That discipline is the difference between a logo that looks good on screen and a brand system that survives the print shop, the sun, daily use, and whoever applies it without supervision.
fg.hard.title: A logo is easy. A system that doesn't break — that's different.
fg.hard.p1: Anyone can deliver a logo as a PNG. What actually protects a brand is that logo working just as well on a multi-meter storefront as on a three-centimeter sticker, on textured cardboard or printed on uniform fabric — without anyone needing to "fix it" every time.
fg.hard.p2: That meant building logo variants for different backgrounds and sizes, defining a palette with clear hierarchy (not just "the brand colors", but which one is background, which is accent, which is detail-only), and documenting everything in brand guidelines so any future print shop or vendor can apply it without asking me every time.
fg.gallery.note: Design finished and production-ready — Fueggo's location hasn't opened yet, so these pieces haven't been printed or installed in the physical world. What you see is the complete system, ready to go straight to print and production on opening day, with no last-minute rework.
fg.recognition.title: What the Fueggo team recognized
fg.impact1.label: Applications designed
fg.impact1.desc: Storefront, packaging, uniform, stationery, and two sticker sets — every piece resolved and production-ready, not just a standalone logo.
fg.impact2.label: Rework on opening day
fg.impact2.desc: Everything documented and print-ready. When Fueggo opens, there's nothing to improvise or redesign at the last minute.
fg.impact3.label: Documented in brand guidelines
fg.impact3.desc: Logo, variants, palette with defined hierarchy and usage rules — any printer or vendor can apply it consistently without asking permission.
fg.impact.note: * Complete system documented and production-ready. Business metrics pending — the location opens soon with this identity applied.
fg.cta.title: Is your brand about to open without a face of its own
fg.cta.sub: If your product is ready but you don't have a brand system to back it up, you're in the same spot Fueggo was: about to compete blind. Tell me about your project. I reply within 24 hours.
jc.diag.label: What the competition has / what La Jungla has
jc.hero.sub: In an area where every nightclub changes its name but looks the same, La Jungla Club needed something no competitor could copy overnight. I built the complete identity system — logo, storefront, menu, uniforms, and flyers — so the brand is recognized before anyone walks through the door.
jc.problem.title: Same format, same vibe. Only the sign changes.
jc.problem.p1: In most nightclubs in the area, the identity ends at the name. The sign changes, the flyer logo changes — but the experience, the colors, and the feeling of walking in are interchangeable from one venue to the next. For someone going out on a Friday, they all blur together.
jc.problem.pull: The client already had something no competitor had: a name with character — La Jungla. What was missing was a visual system capable of honoring that name.
jc.problem.p2: The brief wasn't "make a nice nightclub logo". It was building an identity that translated the concept — jungle, night, intensity — to every surface the brand would appear on: storefront, menu, uniform, event flyer. Something people remembered before they'd ever danced there, not after.
jc.info.role: Complete visual identity, from concept to applied system
jc.info.deliverables: Logo, storefront, menu, uniforms, event flyers
jc.decision.title: The jungle isn't the background. It's the system.
jc.decision.p1: The client arrived with the right idea: a nightclub where jungle meets reggaeton. My job wasn't to invent the concept from scratch — it was to turn that idea into a visual system that holds up on any surface without losing intensity or coherence.
jc.decision.p2: We built something that goes beyond the logo: each resident DJ has their own identity within the brand's universe. It's not a decorative detail — it's a narrative structure that gives La Jungla Club something almost no nightclub in the area has: a visual world of its own, one that can grow with every new event without losing its core.
jc.decision.p3: That decision — thinking in systems, not in standalone pieces — is what separates an identity that dissolves in six months from one that's still recognizable a year later.
jc.hard.title: A strong concept on screen is easy. Surviving five different materials — that's not.
jc.hard.p1: Jungle, night, and intensity work well in an illustration. The real challenge is making that same concept look equally strong printed on vinyl across a multi-meter storefront, embroidered or stamped on uniform fabric, on menu paper under nightclub lighting, and on a flyer competing for attention in a feed saturated with Friday events.
jc.hard.p2: Every surface imposes its own constraints — color, legibility, durability. Designing a system that respected those constraints without diluting the identity on any of them, and without each new piece looking like it came from a different brand, was the real work behind this project.
jc.gallery.note: Every piece is designed for the surface it lives on: the storefront has to stop someone on the street, the uniform has to survive a full shift, the menu has to be readable under low light, and the flyer has to win the scroll in under a second.
jc.cap.facade: Storefront — the first impression, the one that decides if someone walks in
jc.cap.menu: Menu — the brand stays present at every table
jc.cap.uniform: Uniform — the team as a visible part of the concept
jc.recognition.title: A brand you feel before you walk in
jc.impact1.label: Application pieces delivered
jc.impact1.desc: Logo, storefront, menu, uniforms, and event flyers — all cohesive, no rework between pieces.
jc.impact2.label: Visual system, not just a logo
jc.impact2.desc: Jungle and reggaeton translated into a visual language that holds up on vinyl, fabric, paper, and social media.
jc.impact3.label: Real differentiation from the competition
jc.impact3.desc: In a sector where almost every nightclub competes only on name, La Jungla Club now competes with an aesthetic nobody else has.
jc.impact.note: * Qualitative result, based on visual differentiation achieved against direct local competitors. Attendance and conversion metrics were not part of this project's scope.
jc.cta.title: Does your brand compete with a name or with a system
jc.cta.sub: If your business looks the same as the one next door and only the sign changes, that's not a budget problem — it's a system problem. Tell me about your project. I reply within 24 hours.
rs.hero.sub: A clothing brand doesn't just sell garments — it sells a face. Rebel Street arrived with a logo made in Canva and an idea without a system. What's going on the street is this.
rs.problem.title: In streetwear, the brand is the product
rs.problem.p1: Rebel Street was an independent entrepreneur's first own brand. No store, no production yet — just an idea and a logo put together in Canva with AI assistance. Good enough to show a friend. Not good enough to put on a storefront, a clothing tag, or the back of a hoodie someone is going to wear in public.
rs.problem.pull: In urban fashion, nobody buys the garment first — they buy the identity. If that identity looks improvised, the garment feels improvised, regardless of the fabric quality.
rs.problem.p2: The brief wasn't "improve the logo". It was building, from scratch, everything a brand needs to exist coherently before its first sale: a visual system with street credibility — graffiti-influenced, with attitude — but with the discipline to survive applied on storefronts, garments, tags, packaging, and uniforms without falling apart.
rs.info.scope.label: Scope
rs.info.scope: Design and branding — manufacturing and production handled by the client
rs.info.deliverables: Logo, palette, typography, storefront, garments, tags, packaging, staff uniforms
rs.start.title: From an AI logo to a brand system
rs.start.text: This is the real contrast: what existed when the brief arrived, versus the final system already applied. This wasn't a typography tweak — it was building from the ground up what was missing: hierarchy, consistency, and a graphic sensibility that holds up on any surface.
rs.proof.before.label: Starting point
rs.proof.after.label: Final system
rs.hard.title: Graffiti-inspired doesn't mean chaotic
rs.hard.p1: Anyone can drop a spray font and call it "streetwear". The real challenge is different: designing a system with enough street attitude for the right audience to recognize it instantly, but with enough discipline for the same brand to look just as solid on a 4-meter storefront as on a 3-centimeter tag.
rs.hard.p2: That meant defining rules before designing pieces: which graphic elements repeat, how the logo behaves at different scales, which palette stays fixed and where there's room to vary. Without that framework, a "street" system falls apart by the third application — you can see exactly where the rule ended and the improvisation began.
rs.gallery.title: A brand that holds up on any surface
rs.gallery.note: The value of a brand system isn't visible in one piece — it's visible in the tenth piece still feeling like it belongs to the same brand as the first. This is that proof.
rs.cap.facade: Storefront application — the first impression, at real scale
rs.cap.staff: Staff uniforms — the brand is also worn by the people who serve
rs.cap.pattern: Brand pattern — the graphic element that repeats and anchors everything else
rs.cap.label: Tag — the brand confirmed in the smallest detail
rs.cap.bag: Packaging bag — the brand experience starts before the garment is opened
rs.result.title: A brand ready to hit the street
rs.result.p1: Rebel Street went from a practice logo to a complete visual system: 9 pieces applied with the same graphic identity, none of them feeling like the weak link. It's the brand foundation any fashion project needs before its first sale — and proof that I can take a street identity from concept to physical application without losing coherence along the way.
rs.result.placeholder: [REAL DATA: if Rebel Street started selling, launched on social media, or if the client came back for a second phase — let me know and I'll turn it into a real metric or quote here. Without that data, this section stays with what I can actually prove: the system delivered.]
rs.cta.title: Does your brand still look like a practice project
rs.cta.sub: If your logo came out of an AI tool "to get started" and was never updated, your brand is probably still telling that story — even if the product is already ready. Tell me what you're building and I'll tell you what your identity needs to hold up when people start seeing it for real.
ac.diag.label: Recent profile reach
ac.hero.sub: Sports flyers and visual content for the world of motorcycle racing: riders, teams, and events. My own brand — born from understanding racing culture from the inside, and translating that adrenaline into pieces people actually want to share.
ac.why.title: Motorcycle racing is lived with intensity. Its content almost never is.
ac.why.p1: The motorcycle world is full of passion: riders training daily, teams competing every weekend, events that move entire communities. But when it comes time to communicate — a race announcement, a rider introduction, an event — most end up with a generic template that conveys none of that adrenaline.
ac.why.pull: A sports brand that looks weak communicates that the team is weak. In a sector where image is identity, a piece without force is a missed opportunity.
ac.why.p2: AcelerArte was born from that: from being part of the culture and understanding that a motorsport flyer isn't "a nice design" — it's a statement of attitude. Speed, risk, precision — that's what every piece needs to make someone feel before they read a single word.
ac.info.type.label: Type
ac.info.sector.label: Sector
ac.info.role.value: Identity, art direction and design of every piece
ac.info.for.label: For whom
ac.criterion.title: I don't design a flyer. I design what it feels like to hit 200.
ac.criterion.p1: Every piece starts with one question: what does someone need to feel when they see it in their feed among hundreds of posts? The answer — tension, power, motion — drives everything: the composition has to have speed even when the image is still, the typography has to look sharp and aggressive, and the color has to carry energy without falling into the cliché of "generic red and black".
ac.criterion.p2: That's why I integrate rider photography with dramatic lighting, motion lines, and a typographic system that feels pulled from the MotoGP paddock. It's not decoration — it's translating each rider's or team's identity into an image they want on their profile, and that their community wants to share.
ac.gallery.title: The work, piece by piece
ac.gallery.note: Sports flyers for motorcycle riders and teams. Each one with its own color identity and attitude, but with the same visual signature: high impact, competition aesthetics, built for social media.
ac.reaction.title: The work moved the right community
ac.impact1.label: Profile visits in 28 days
ac.impact1.desc: Recent reach for the AcelerArte account, driven by the pieces — not paid ads.
ac.impact2.label: Interactions in the period
ac.impact2.desc: Likes, comments, and messages from a real motorcycle community — not bought followers.
ac.impact3.label: Reposts from recognized riders
ac.impact3.desc: Riders like Mario González and Angelo Bonilla reposted their pieces in stories and messaged to congratulate the work.
ac.impact.note: * Instagram data (last 28 days). Additionally, brand accounts in the sector such as CF Moto and Honda have interacted with the pieces. Real numbers, verifiable on the profile.
ac.cta.title: Does your team or event deserve to look as good as the track
ac.cta.sub: If you're a rider, manage a team, or organize a motorcycle event and you're tired of generic templates — let's talk. I design flyers and content that look like racing feels. I reply within 24 hours.
sv.hero.sub: Three disciplines, one person. No handoffs between teams, no middlemen — you talk directly to the person designing and building your project.
sv.s1.title: Web design and development
sv.s1.desc: Fast, custom-built sites — designed and coded by the same person. No templates, no third-party dependencies.
sv.s1.li2: Clean code: HTML/CSS/JS or custom WordPress
sv.s1.li3: Technical SEO and PageSpeed optimization from day one
sv.s2.title: Visual identity and branding
sv.s2.desc: A memorable brand isn't improvised. Logo, brand guidelines, and real-world applications — built with intention.
sv.s2.li1: Professional logo in all its variants
sv.s2.li2: Brand guidelines: palette, typography and usage rules
sv.s2.li3: Real applications: packaging, uniforms, social media, storefronts
sv.s3.title: Graphic design and social media
sv.s3.desc: Visual pieces that communicate, sell, and stick — with a considered aesthetic, not improvised for the scroll.
sv.s3.li1: Social media content with a coherent visual system
sv.s3.li2: Graphic pieces for campaigns and promotions
sv.s3.li3: Art direction applied to every deliverable
sv.why.title: One person, not a team taking turns
sv.why.p1: An agency has three people between your idea and the result: the one who sells, the one who coordinates, and the one who executes. Every layer adds time and dilutes what you asked for.
sv.why.p2: With me there are no layers. You talk directly to the person designing and coding — the same judgment that defines your visual identity is the one writing your site's code. Nothing gets lost in the handoff.
sv.why.p3: Projects from $500 USD. A clear proposal with scope, timeline, and price before we start — no surprises along the way.
sv.stat1: PageSpeed Insights, Orbidental — measured in production
sv.stat2: Products photographed and catalogued for e-commerce
sv.stat3: Projects delivered with no middlemen, direct with the client
sv.cta.title: Which of the three does your project need
sv.cta.sub: Not sure? Tell me what you need and I'll point you in the right direction. I reply within 24 hours.
ab.hero.title: The person behind the work
ab.hero.sub: Designer, developer, and visual strategist. All in one — no 5-person team splitting your budget.
ab.01.title: Origin
ab.01.body: I didn't learn in a classroom — I learned by building real things, through trial and error, since I was 13: over 5 years of accumulating real mistakes and fixing them, not class exercises. That has a direct consequence for you: I don't sell textbook theory. Every design or code decision I make has already failed and been corrected before, in a real project.
ab.02.title: What I do
ab.02.body: For over 2 years I've managed everything digital for a real company: Meta and Google Ads, social media design, print materials, product photography, and an e-commerce store with 3,000+ products. I migrated their site to clean code and took PageSpeed from 40 to 97 — no agency, no endless meetings, no translator between "what the business needs" and "what the developer understands". What for an agency means 3 separate departments, for me is a day's work.
ab.stat3: Years managing a full digital operation
ab.03.title: How I work
ab.03.body: You work directly with me — not an account manager translating your messages, not a junior executing what someone else decided. That means fast responses, clear decisions made by the same person who will build them, and a project that moves without friction. I work 100% remotely with clients across Latin America, Europe, and the United States.
ab.inf.title: Off the screen
ab.inf.body: You'll find me on the bike. Motorcycling is my other passion — the same mindset I apply to work: read the terrain before you move, stay in control even when everything's happening fast, and always forward.
ab.cta.title: Ready to start a project
ab.cta.sub: Tell me what you need. I reply within 24 hours — directly me, not a form that lands in an unreviewed inbox.
iv.eyebrow: SERVICE · REMOTE, COLOMBIA AND THE WORLD
iv.hero.title: Visual
iv.hero.sub: A memorable brand isn't improvised. It's built with intention from the very first stroke.
iv.queEs.title: Your brand is the first thing clients see
iv.queEs.p1: A solid visual identity makes your company look professional, coherent, and memorable across every touchpoint — from the logo to the packaging, from social media to the storefront.
iv.queEs.p2: I work with brands that want to genuinely stand out. No generic AI logos or templates — every project starts by understanding the business, the target client, and the competition.
iv.queEs.p3: I've done it for restaurants, nightclubs, and clothing brands — and in every case the client recognized their idea materialized into something they never could have imagined alone.

dg.why.title: No agency, no telephone game
dg.why.p1: With an agency you talk to an account exec who then explains to a designer what you already explained. The result arrives late, costs more, and isn't what you asked for.
dg.why.p2: With me you talk directly to the person designing. I understand the brief from the first conversation, deliver fast, and adjust without drama.

fp.hero.title: Product
fp.hero.sub: A good photo doesn't just show the product. It shows its value, builds trust, and converts.
fp.queEs.title: The customer can't touch your product — but they can see it
fp.queEs.p1: In e-commerce, the photo is the product. A buyer makes their decision in seconds based solely on what they see. A dark, blurry, or generic photo can cost your business sales every single day.
fp.queEs.p2: I work with natural and artificial light, neutral or styled backgrounds, and professional Photoshop editing so every product looks exactly as it should.
fp.queEs.p3: I've photographed and edited over 3,000 products for Orbidental — each with multiple angles, light correction, and web optimization.
fp.cta.title: Do your products deserve better photos
fp.cta.sub: Tell me how many products and what you need. I reply within 24 hours.
diseno-web.hero.title: Web design that works
diseno-web.hero.sub: Professional websites for businesses that want to grow. Fast, responsive, and optimized from day one.
diseno-web.queEs.title: Your website is your hardest-working salesperson
diseno-web.queEs.p1: A well-built website works for you 24 hours a day. When a potential client searches for you on Google, the first thing they see is your site — and within 3 seconds they've already made a decision.
diseno-web.queEs.p2: I build sites in pure HTML, CSS and JavaScript or on WordPress with Kadence — no slow builders, no bloated code. Every project is optimized to load fast, look great on any device, and rank on Google.
diseno-web.queEs.p3: The Orbidental result speaks for itself: from a PageSpeed of 40 to 97, with an e-commerce store of 3,000+ products that now runs fast and error-free.
diseno-web.incluye.1.title: Custom design
diseno-web.incluye.1.desc: No templates. Every site is designed from scratch based on the brand and the client's goals.
diseno-web.incluye.2.title: Responsive and fast
diseno-web.incluye.2.desc: Works perfectly on mobile, tablet, and desktop. PageSpeed optimized from the start.
diseno-web.proceso.title: How I work
diseno-web.proceso.1.title: 01 — Briefing
diseno-web.proceso.1.desc: We talk about the project, goals, audience, and competition. Without this there's no proposal.
diseno-web.proceso.2.title: 02 — Design
diseno-web.proceso.2.desc: I design the structure and look of the site. Proposals are presented and adjusted until perfect.
diseno-web.proceso.3.title: 03 — Development
diseno-web.proceso.3.desc: I build the site in clean code. No templates, no unnecessary plugins, no bloated code.
diseno-web.proceso.4.title: 04 — Optimization
diseno-web.proceso.4.desc: Technical SEO, PageSpeed, accessibility, and cross-device testing before launch.
diseno-web.proceso.5.title: 05 — Delivery
diseno-web.proceso.5.desc: The site goes live. I hand over access, documentation, and post-launch support.

pp.title: Privacy policy
pp.meta: Last updated: June 2026 · Controller: Alexander Caro
pp.s1.p: The controller of personal data collected through this website is:
pp.label.name: Name:
pp.s1.activity: Freelance web designer and frontend developer
pp.s2.p: Through the contact form on this site I collect the following data:
pp.s2.note: I do not collect sensitive data such as financial information, identity documents, or health data.
pp.s3.p: The data you submit through the form is used exclusively to:
pp.s3.note: I do not use your data to send advertising, unsolicited newsletters, nor do I share it with third parties for commercial purposes.
pp.s4.h: 4. Who I share your data with
pp.s4.note: I do not sell, rent, or transfer your data to third parties.
pp.s5.h: 5. How long I keep your data
pp.contact.q: Do you have any questions about how your data is used?
pp.contact.a: Write to me at hosoyalexander@gmail.com — I'll reply within 24 hours.
pp.s3.li1: Respond to your inquiry or quote request
pp.s3.li2: Coordinate the details of a potential project
pp.s3.li3: Contact you if I need additional information to prepare a proposal
pp.s4.p1: Form messages are processed by Web3Forms (web3forms.com), an external service that acts as an intermediary to deliver messages to my inbox. Web3Forms does not store your data permanently or use it for other purposes.
pp.s4.p2: The site is hosted on Vercel (vercel.com), which logs basic technical server data (IP address, browser type, pages visited) for operational and security purposes. This is standard practice for any web hosting service.
pp.s5.p1: I keep received messages for as long as necessary to handle your inquiry and, if we work together, for the duration of the project and up to 2 years after it closes, in case any follow-up questions arise.
pp.s5.p2: If we don't end up working together, I delete your information once it's no longer needed to respond to your inquiry.
pp.s6.p: This site does not use tracking cookies or analytics tools like Google Analytics. Vercel may set minimal technical cookies required for server operation. These cookies do not identify users individually and are not used for advertising purposes.
pp.s7.li1: Access the data I hold about you
pp.s7.li3: Request its deletion when it's no longer necessary
pp.s7.li4: Object to its processing at any time
pp.s7.note: To exercise any of these rights, write to me directly at hosoyalexander@gmail.com and I'll respond within 5 business days.
pp.s8.p: This site uses HTTPS across all its pages and security headers configured on the server. Messages are transmitted in encrypted form between your browser and the services involved.
pp.s9.p: If I make any significant changes to this policy, I'll update the "last updated" date at the top of this page. I recommend checking it occasionally if you have an active inquiry.
